summary:
The Song Dynasty (960–1279 AD) was founded when the military commander Zhao Kuangyin (Emperor Taizu) ended the [[event:2157|Five Dynasties period]] by staging a coup — reportedly engineered by his generals who dressed him in an imperial robe while he slept — and went on to reunify most of China. The Song dynasty is considered one of China's most sophisticated — an era of extraordinary commercial development, urbanization, technological innovation, and intellectual achievement. Song China had the world's most advanced economy, with paper money ([[event:2217|Chinese paper money]]), joint-stock companies, large-scale manufacturing, and an urban population proportion unmatched until industrial Europe.

The Song period produced [[event:2158|woodblock printing]] and the world's first movable type (Bi Sheng, 1040 AD), [[event:2160|gunpowder weapons]], magnetic compasses for navigation, and porcelain of unsurpassed quality. The imperial examination system expanded to produce a genuine meritocratic bureaucracy. The southern Song (after losing northern China to the Jurchen Jin dynasty in 1127) continued as a culturally brilliant state centered on Hangzhou, which Marco Polo would later describe as the greatest city in the world. Song China is sometimes considered the world's first proto-modern commercial society — its merchant class, urban culture, and market economy anticipate European developments by centuries. The dynasty was finally destroyed by the Mongol conquests under Kublai Khan (1279 AD).
